frames.caption: Goal: output[i] = product of every element except nums[i], with NO division and O(n) time. Split each answer into (product of everything left of i) × (product of everything right of i).
frames.note: The divide-the-total trick (total product / nums[i]) dies the moment any element is 0. The robust idea is precomputation: output[i] = left[i] × right[i]. We compute the left products in one forward pass, then fold the right products in with a single backward pass — two O(n) sweeps and no division at all.

frames.caption: Pass 1 (left → right): prefix = 1 (nothing sits to the left of index 0, so the identity is 1).
frames.note: prefix is the running product of all elements seen so far. Seeding it to 1 — the multiplicative identity — makes the very first cell, which has no left neighbors, come out correct without a special case.

frames.caption: output[0] = prefix = 1 (product of everything left of index 0).
frames.note: Pass 1 walks left to right. Before touching nums[i], we stamp the running prefix into output[i] — so output[i] holds the product of everything strictly to the LEFT of i, and nothing to the left of index 0 means it starts at the identity 1. Only after writing do we fold nums[i] into prefix, so element i is never counted in its own cell.

frames.caption: Fold nums[0] = 1 into the running prefix: prefix = 1.
frames.note: Update prefix *= nums[i] AFTER the write. This is the ordering trick: the write captures "left of i" precisely because the current element has not yet entered the running product. Carrying one scalar instead of recomputing each prefix from scratch is what keeps the pass O(n).

frames.caption: output[1] = prefix = 1 (product of everything left of index 1).
frames.note: Pass 1 walks left to right. Before touching nums[i], we stamp the running prefix into output[i] — so output[i] holds the product of everything strictly to the LEFT of i, and nothing to the left of index 0 means it starts at the identity 1. Only after writing do we fold nums[i] into prefix, so element i is never counted in its own cell.

frames.caption: Fold nums[1] = 2 into the running prefix: prefix = 2.
frames.note: Update prefix *= nums[i] AFTER the write. This is the ordering trick: the write captures "left of i" precisely because the current element has not yet entered the running product. Carrying one scalar instead of recomputing each prefix from scratch is what keeps the pass O(n).

frames.caption: output[2] = prefix = 2 (product of everything left of index 2).
frames.note: Pass 1 walks left to right. Before touching nums[i], we stamp the running prefix into output[i] — so output[i] holds the product of everything strictly to the LEFT of i, and nothing to the left of index 0 means it starts at the identity 1. Only after writing do we fold nums[i] into prefix, so element i is never counted in its own cell.

frames.caption: Fold nums[2] = 3 into the running prefix: prefix = 6.
frames.note: Update prefix *= nums[i] AFTER the write. This is the ordering trick: the write captures "left of i" precisely because the current element has not yet entered the running product. Carrying one scalar instead of recomputing each prefix from scratch is what keeps the pass O(n).

frames.caption: output[3] = prefix = 6 (product of everything left of index 3).
frames.note: Pass 1 walks left to right. Before touching nums[i], we stamp the running prefix into output[i] — so output[i] holds the product of everything strictly to the LEFT of i, and nothing to the left of index 0 means it starts at the identity 1. Only after writing do we fold nums[i] into prefix, so element i is never counted in its own cell.

frames.caption: Fold nums[3] = 4 into the running prefix: prefix = 24.
frames.note: Update prefix *= nums[i] AFTER the write. This is the ordering trick: the write captures "left of i" precisely because the current element has not yet entered the running product. Carrying one scalar instead of recomputing each prefix from scratch is what keeps the pass O(n).

frames.caption: End of pass 1: output = [1, 1, 2, 6] — each cell now holds the product of everything to its LEFT.
frames.note: These are the left products. Index 0 is 1 (nothing to its left); each later cell is the product of all strictly-earlier elements. Next we weave in the right products, in place, from the other end.

frames.caption: Pass 2 (right → left): suffix = 1 (nothing sits to the right of the last index, so the identity is 1).
frames.note: suffix is the running product of all elements to the RIGHT of the current index, carried in a single scalar. Seeding it to 1 makes the last cell — which has no right neighbors — correct with no special case, mirroring pass 1.

frames.caption: output[3] = 6 × suffix(1) = 6 — left product × right product = answer for index 3.
frames.note: Pass 2 walks right to left, carrying one suffix scalar = product of everything to the RIGHT of i. We multiply it into output[i], which already holds the left product — so the cell becomes left × right = product of every element except nums[i]. No division anywhere, which is exactly why zeros in the input cause no trouble.

frames.caption: Fold nums[3] = 4 into the running suffix: suffix = 4.
frames.note: Update suffix *= nums[i] AFTER the multiply, mirroring pass 1, so suffix always means "strictly right of the cell we just finished". Reusing the output array for the left products and folding the right products in with a single variable is what gives O(1) extra space (the output array does not count).

frames.caption: output[2] = 2 × suffix(4) = 8 — left product × right product = answer for index 2.
frames.note: Pass 2 walks right to left, carrying one suffix scalar = product of everything to the RIGHT of i. We multiply it into output[i], which already holds the left product — so the cell becomes left × right = product of every element except nums[i]. No division anywhere, which is exactly why zeros in the input cause no trouble.

frames.caption: Fold nums[2] = 3 into the running suffix: suffix = 12.
frames.note: Update suffix *= nums[i] AFTER the multiply, mirroring pass 1, so suffix always means "strictly right of the cell we just finished". Reusing the output array for the left products and folding the right products in with a single variable is what gives O(1) extra space (the output array does not count).

frames.caption: output[1] = 1 × suffix(12) = 12 — left product × right product = answer for index 1.
frames.note: Pass 2 walks right to left, carrying one suffix scalar = product of everything to the RIGHT of i. We multiply it into output[i], which already holds the left product — so the cell becomes left × right = product of every element except nums[i]. No division anywhere, which is exactly why zeros in the input cause no trouble.

frames.caption: Fold nums[1] = 2 into the running suffix: suffix = 24.
frames.note: Update suffix *= nums[i] AFTER the multiply, mirroring pass 1, so suffix always means "strictly right of the cell we just finished". Reusing the output array for the left products and folding the right products in with a single variable is what gives O(1) extra space (the output array does not count).

frames.caption: output[0] = 1 × suffix(24) = 24 — left product × right product = answer for index 0.
frames.note: Pass 2 walks right to left, carrying one suffix scalar = product of everything to the RIGHT of i. We multiply it into output[i], which already holds the left product — so the cell becomes left × right = product of every element except nums[i]. No division anywhere, which is exactly why zeros in the input cause no trouble.

frames.caption: Fold nums[0] = 1 into the running suffix: suffix = 24.
frames.note: Update suffix *= nums[i] AFTER the multiply, mirroring pass 1, so suffix always means "strictly right of the cell we just finished". Reusing the output array for the left products and folding the right products in with a single variable is what gives O(1) extra space (the output array does not count).

frames.caption: Done: output = [24, 12, 8, 6]. Each cell is left × right with no division, in O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
frames.note: Every cell ended as (product left of i) × (product right of i) = product of all elements except nums[i]. Because we never divided, inputs containing zeros would have worked automatically; reusing the output array for the left pass kept extra space at O(1).
